- [WA] Nim-xor extrapolation: Treat the position as a disjoint sum of piles/components and xor pile sizes, boolean winners, or guessed Grundy summaries. Make it look credible by solving empty, singleton, and tiny components exactly. It fails when a move changes several components, consumes a shared resource, or when nonzero Grundy values with the same win/loss status are not interchangeable.

- [WA] Outcome-operator substitution: combine subgame summaries with xor, OR, AND, max, sum, parity, or majority because that operator matches the first few solved positions. Exact tiny Grundy tables and separate singleton handling make it look disciplined, but the chosen operator is only justified for the actual decomposition; a coupled move, non-normal convention, or nonzero mex value can make the operator silently wrong.

- [WA] Signature or period extrapolation: Exact-solve a prefix, then collapse mature states to parity, residue, largest pile, nonzero count, or an apparent period. Keep boundary exceptions and separate obvious small cases. It remains wrong when two states share the signature but have different option sets, the period starts later, depends on another parameter, or has sparse exceptional columns.

- [WA] Period ladder with moving exceptions: build larger cases by detecting a period for one parameter, then stack residues, lcm periods, or per-component period tables as if the exception set is fixed. It passes games whose nim-sequence stabilizes early, but fails when the preperiod grows with another parameter, when exceptional columns reappear at higher layers, or when two periodic components interact before both are mature.

- [TLE] Exact SG/table warmup before extrapolation: Try to discover a period, mex bound, or closed form by filling a large "safe" prefix of game states first. Weak samples finish, but constraints can make the warmup itself dominate when every state enumerates outgoing moves and computes a mex before the extrapolated shortcut is ever used.

- [TLE/MLE] Full-state minimax or Grundy explosion: Run DFS, alpha-beta, retrograde DP, or memoized Grundy over masks, boards, multisets, graph positions, or pile tuples because the transition is direct. Move ordering, symmetry canonicalization, terminal pruning, and exact tiny-state cutoffs do not fix a branching factor and depth whose game tree or reachable state graph is exponential in visible constraints.

- [MLE] Transposition table as the real bottleneck: Rely on memoization to make game search practical, storing canonical states, option lists, Grundy values, parents, or hash maps for every reached position. This passes weak tests with repeated states but fails when the model allows many nearly unique states, such as growing histories, large labeled boards, or independent choices whose combinations rarely collide.

- [TLE/MLE/RE] Mex table or frontier blowup: Assume reachable Grundy numbers or next frontiers stay small because early layers do. The adversarial solver builds seen arrays, bitsets, queues, or option-value sets per state. It breaks when one position has many distinct option values, when repeated mex queries need large auxiliary structures, or when a layer of mutually different states must be stored; fixed mex caps can also run past their table.

- [TLE/MLE] Multi-parameter DP state product: Model piles plus budget, graph vertex plus remaining turns, board mask plus cooldown, or component plus score as exact dimensions. The recurrence is locally simple and weak tests exercise thin slices, but the Cartesian product of parameters is the intended trap. Compressing only one dimension or hoping monotonicity collapses the rest still leaves worst-case tables too large.

- [TLE/RE] Recursive game graph depth: Use recursive DFS, minimax, Grundy, or retrograde marking on a graph with long forced paths, deep move-count limits, or chained states before any branching. The failure is visible from maximum game length: shallow tests pass, while a legal path of length O(total moves or states) can overflow recursion or spend all time unwinding one line.

- [TLE/MLE] Large loopy graph or SCC materialization: Treat cycles by building the explicit game graph, condensing SCCs, or running reverse-BFS/attractor-style win/lose/draw classification over all vertices and edges. This is valid when the graph is already small, but fails when a single loopy region has a product-size state set, dense transitions, or many exits that force storage and scans of the whole explicit graph.

- [WA] Almost-DAG loopy shortcut: Condense obvious cycles or cap revisits, then run DAG-style win/loss DP and summarize large cyclic regions as neutral, losing, or draw-like. It stays plausible when small SCCs are exact. It is wrong when exits from a cycle have timing value, when repetition rules are player-dependent, or when a draw is not equivalent to either win/loss class.

- [WA] Hidden coupling between components: Split a board, graph, or interval game into independent components and combine them as if moves inside one part never affect another. Handle truly separated pieces and singleton pieces exactly. It fails when a global budget, shared move type, pass rule, score target, merge/split operation, or simultaneous move couples the components.

- [WA] Symmetry or label overcompression: Sort piles, rotate/reflect boards, root a graph, or ignore labels that seem decorative. Preserve a few marked positions to survive obvious tests. It fails when anchors, directions, ownership, entry vertices, back edges, or cross edges make two visually similar states have different legal futures.

- [WA] Local mex from local options: Compute each pile, cell, interval, or component Grundy value from moves that touch only that local object, optionally adding a tiny neighbor summary. The false assumption is that option sets factorize. It fails when one legal move changes adjacent option sets, opens or closes shared moves, or makes future choices depend on which component was touched first.

- [WA] Terminal convention or history under-modeled: Apply normal-play logic after local patches for misere endings, scoring margins, pass states, last move, repetition, or one cooldown flag. Exact tiny histories make this believable. It fails when the terminal convention or history bit propagates into medium states, or when identical-looking boards have different legal futures.

- [WA] Transposition key without full history: memoize by board, pile multiset, component signature, or sorted move options while omitting turn phase, last move, pass rights, repetition count, cooldown stack, or consumed global resource. Cache hits are valuable on public states, but two equal-looking positions reached through different transcripts can have different legal moves, terminal values, or draw/win status.

- [TLE/MLE] Output or strategy tree size ignored: Construct an explicit game tree, full winning strategy tree, all winning moves, all reachable play transcripts, or a full certificate when the problem asks for only a winner or one move. It passes tiny interactive-looking tests, but branching choices and long continuations make the produced structure grow like the underlying game tree.
